journal-title-group.journal-title: Journal of General and Family Medicine

title-group.article-title: Clinical Characteristics of Patients Initially Suspected of Long COVID: A Case Series From a Specialized Outpatient Clinic

aff.named-content: Department of General Internal Medicine and Clinical Laboratory
aff.institution: National Center of Neurology and Psychiatry National Center Hospital
National Center of Neurology and Psychiatry National Center Hospital

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Journal of General and Family Medicine published by John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd on behalf of Japan Primary Care Association.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Sequelae of the acute phase of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID‐19), termed long COVID, are characterized by a variety of symptoms, including neurological manifestations. Diagnosing long COVID requires excluding alternative conditions that could explain the symptoms. The role of primary care physicians is considered essential in managing long COVID, particularly during the initial screening phase.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This observational, retrospective, single‐center study was conducted at an outpatient clinic from 1 June 2021 to 31 December 2024. We confirmed final diagnoses for patients suspected of having long COVID and included those ultimately diagnosed with other conditions. Clinical data—including symptoms, demographic characteristics, results of clinical examinations, and final diagnoses—were collected.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: In total, 44 patients were diagnosed with alternative conditions. Of these, 30 were classified as having post‐acute sequelae of SARS‐CoV‐2 mimic, and 14 patients (2.2% of those who believed they had long COVID and visited our clinic) were diagnosed with other diseases. The median age at the time of their clinic visit was 48 years (range, 42.5–61.5). Diagnoses included collagen diseases (5 patients, 35.7%) and central nervous system disorders (5 patients, 35.7%), among others. Weakness was the most common symptom. In contrast to long COVID, fatigue was less frequently reported in this group.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: We identified a variety of alternative diagnoses among patients suspected of having long COVID. We recommend that primary care physicians exercise caution when diagnosing long COVID, particularly in patients who do not report fatigue.

institution-wrap.institution: National Center of Neurology and Psychiatry
Japan Agency for Medical Research and Development

p.mixed-citation: M. Ohira , T. Osada , H. Kimura , T. Sano , and M. Takao , “Clinical Characteristics of Patients Initially Suspected of Long COVID: A Case Series From a Specialized Outpatient Clinic,” Journal of General and Family Medicine 27, no. 1 (2026): e70081, 10.1002/jgf2.70081.

fn.p: Funding: This work was supported by National Center of Neurology and Psychiatry, 6‐8, 5‐S13; Japan Agency for Medical Research and Development, JP25dk0307125.
Guarantor of the article: Masaki Takao

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS‐CoV‐2), the virus responsible for coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID‐19), led to a global pandemic. COVID‐19 is well known not only for causing respiratory dysfunction during the acute phase but also for producing a range of long‐term symptoms affecting various organs [1]. These long‐term symptoms have not been clearly defined, and multiple terms and definitions are still in use [2]. For convenience, we refer to them as “long COVID” in this report. A recent meta‐analysis reported a 41.79% pooled global prevalence of long COVID [3]. Even patients with a history of mild or asymptomatic SARS‐CoV‐2 infection may develop long COVID, and primary care physicians must include it in the differential diagnosis for a variety of symptoms [4]. One review emphasized that the role of primary care physicians is especially critical in the early screening of patients with suspected long COVID [5].

One well‐known definition of long COVID is the World Health Organization (WHO) Delphi consensus criteria (the WHO criteria), which require that symptoms cannot be explained by an alternative diagnosis [6]. Confirming an alternative diagnosis and conducting differential diagnoses are recognized as important steps in the assessment of patients with suspected long COVID [7]. Standard primary care evaluations are recommended to include the identification of other potential pathologies unrelated to the viral infection, as well as pre‐existing conditions or symptoms, supported by appropriate complementary tests to rule out other etiologies [8].

The range of possible differential diagnoses for long COVID spans a wide variety of diseases. However, in primary care settings, the ability to perform medical tests to exclude other conditions may be limited due to each institution's physical and economic constraints. Therefore, it may be helpful for primary care physicians to be familiar in advance with specific diseases or conditions that can present similarly to long COVID, allowing for a more effective diagnostic process. To date, however, no previous report has detailed the differential diagnoses for long COVID in Japan.

In 2021, our institute—the National Center of Neurology and Psychiatry (NCNP)—established an outpatient clinic specializing in long COVID because of the wide range of neurological and neuropsychiatric symptoms observed in these patients [9, 10]. In this report, we present a series of cases in which long COVID was ruled out following the confirmation of alternative diagnoses, with the aim of providing primary care physicians with useful information for making accurate diagnoses of long COVID.

sec.title: Materials and Methods
sec.p: We performed a single‐center retrospective review at the Department of General Internal Medicine of the NCNP, Japan. Electronic medical records of patients who visited the clinic between 1 June 2021 and 31 December 2024 were reviewed. All patients were over 15 years of age. At our clinic, neurological examinations, blood tests, and imaging studies were performed to exclude other conditions according to each patient's symptoms.

Patients who reported symptoms after vaccination were excluded, and we confirmed the applicability of the WHO criteria for long COVID. Confirmation of past SARS‐CoV‐2 infection by polymerase chain reaction or antigen testing was also required.

Patients without a documented history of COVID‐19 who had not undergone antibody testing at our outpatient clinic were excluded because their prior infection status could not be determined. We also excluded patients who did not meet other parts of the WHO criteria, apart from the requirement that symptoms could not be explained by an alternative diagnosis. Specifically, patients who visited our outpatient clinic within 3 months of COVID‐19 onset and those whose symptoms appeared to predate the infection were excluded. Therefore, we included patients whose symptoms were ultimately explained by an alternative diagnosis rather than long COVID. Data collected included their symptoms, demographic information, clinical examination results, and final alternative diagnoses.

Standard descriptive statistics were used to summarize patient characteristics. Continuous variables are expressed as median (interquartile range: 25%–75%), and categorical variables are expressed as frequencies and percentages.
This study was approved by the NCNP Clinical Research Review Committee (No. A2021‐101).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: In total, 976 patient medical records were screened. First, 325 patients were excluded because they visited our clinic for symptoms unrelated to COVID‐19—most commonly, symptoms following vaccination. Second, 590 patients met the WHO definition of long COVID and had a confirmed past SARS‐CoV‐2 infection as shown by polymerase chain reaction or antibody testing during the acute phase. Their symptoms could not be explained by an alternative diagnosis. Third, 17 patients were excluded: 8 lacked confirmation of past infection and were not tested for antibodies at our clinic, 2 visited the clinic before 3 months had passed since their COVID‐19 onset, and 7 reported that their symptoms predated COVID‐19. After these exclusions, 44 patients remained. Of these, 30 were diagnosed with post‐acute sequelae of SARS‐CoV‐2 mimic (hereafter referred to as “the mimic”), a condition in which patients had symptoms indistinguishable from long COVID and a history of probable—but unconfirmed—SARS‐CoV‐2 infection during the acute phase, with negative anti‐nucleotide SARS‐CoV‐2 antibodies during the chronic phase [11].

Finally, 14 patients—7 men and 7 women, representing 2.2% of those who believed they had long COVID and visited our clinic—were diagnosed with conditions other than long COVID (Figure 1). The median age at the time of their clinic visit was 48 years (range, 42.5–61.5 years). These patients visited our clinic between January 2022 and May 2023, having experienced the acute phase of COVID‐19 prior to 2022. Only two patients required hospitalization during the acute phase. Pre‐existing comorbidities were hypertension in four patients, psychiatric disorders in three, and spinal canal stenosis in two. One patient each had a history of cerebral hemorrhage, nephrotic syndrome, ulcerative colitis, and atopic dermatitis. The patient with nephrotic syndrome was receiving steroid treatment at the time of the acute phase. Details of their diagnoses are summarized in Tables 1 and 2. The most common symptom was weakness or difficulty with movement, observed in 6 patients (42.9%), followed by numbness or pain, and fatigue (Table 3). By contrast, among the 590 patients with long COVID, the most common symptom was fatigue (287 patients, 48.6%), followed by memory disturbance (151, 25.6%) and headache (110, 18.6%).

caption.p: Study flowchart illustrating the selection process for cases included in this study. COVID, coronavirus disease; SARS‐CoV‐2, severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2; WHO, World Health Organization.
Summary of alternative diseases and symptoms in each patient.

tr.td: Myositis with positive U1 ribonucleoprotein antibody

caption.p: Final diagnoses in patients initially suspected of long COVID (N = 14).

tr.td: Myositis with positive U1 ribonucleoprotein antibody

caption.p: Comparison of symptoms between patients diagnosed with other diseases and those with long COVID.

sec.p: Among the 14 patients, blood tests were conducted in 11, including 6 who were diagnosed with either a collagen disease or an infectious disease. However, the white blood cell counts of these 6 patients were within the normal range. Only 2 patients showed elevated C‐reactive protein levels, with values of 4.65 and 1.01 mg/dL, respectively.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In this study, the number of patients diagnosed with diseases other than long COVID was relatively small—14 in total. Collagen diseases and central nervous system disorders were among the more common findings, but no single disease emerged as the most likely differential diagnosis for long COVID. Additionally, standard blood tests did not appear to be useful in distinguishing these alternative diagnoses from long COVID. Even patients with collagen or infectious diseases often showed minimal abnormalities in standard inflammatory markers.

We identified only 14 cases with alternative diagnoses at our clinic, which specializes in long COVID. Certain categories of disease appeared more frequently than others in this study. Most of the conditions in our case series were reasonable differential diagnoses for long COVID, based on previous research. Various immune‐mediated manifestations have been observed following COVID‐19 [8]. For example, cases of inflammatory arthritis, including rheumatoid arthritis, have been reported to increase after COVID‐19 [12]. Most of these cases occurred within the first year after infection, which may have led some patients to mistakenly attribute their symptoms to long COVID. The association between COVID‐19 and Sjögren's syndrome has also been suggested in case reports, and prior infections have been recognized as potential triggers for its development [13]. Although causal mechanisms remain unclear, post‐COVID parkinsonism and Alzheimer's disease have also been reported [14, 15]. Although fungal sinusitis mimicking long COVID has not yet been widely documented, an increase in acute invasive fungal sinusitis was reported during the pandemic [16].

A uniform set of examinations to exclude alternative diagnoses was not performed for all patients at our clinic because evaluations for this purpose were tailored to each patient according to their chief complaint, medical history, and previous test results from other hospitals. On the basis of the symptoms observed at our clinic, the conditions that were relatively often excluded were collagen diseases, central nervous system disorders such as dementia, thyroid dysfunction, myasthenia gravis, and zinc deficiency, among others [10]. Previous reports of patients with long COVID in Japan who were ultimately diagnosed with conditions other than long COVID identified endocrine and metabolic diseases, hematological diseases, and respiratory diseases as the most common alternative diagnoses [17]. However, it was also noted that endocrine and metabolic diseases showed the weakest association with specific symptoms. Thus, diagnoses of organic diseases unrelated to the symptoms of long COVID could have been overlooked in our clinic. Nevertheless, the WHO criteria require the absence of an alternative diagnosis that can explain the symptoms. In addition, because our hospital specializes in neurological and psychiatric disorders, the symptom distribution in our clinic is expected to differ from that of the earlier report. Therefore, we consider it unlikely that unrelated concurrent diseases had a major impact on the diagnosis of long COVID in our setting. The clinical importance of screening for endocrine and metabolic disorders remains uncertain in the context of diagnosing long COVID under the WHO criteria.

In this study, 30 patients were identified as having the mimic. They were excluded because they were highly unlikely to have had COVID‐19. These patients believed they had long COVID, but they had no positive test results during the acute phase and later showed negative anti‐SARS‐CoV‐2 antibody tests in the chronic phase. This indicated that the likelihood of prior COVID‐19 infection was extremely low [11]. According to the WHO definition, these cases could technically be classified as long COVID because it allows for a history of probable SARS‐CoV‐2 infection [6]. The definition from the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine in the United States includes a similar provision [18]. However, from a theoretical standpoint, the symptoms in these mimic cases were unrelated to COVID‐19. Therefore, we excluded them from this study.

Our study suggests that there are a few key diseases to consider and rule out when primary care physicians diagnose long COVID. However, standard blood tests may not be effective for this purpose. This finding aligns with previous reports indicating that most routine clinical laboratory values—such as the leukocyte count, C‐reactive protein level, sodium level, and potassium level—are not clinically useful biomarkers for long COVID [19]. Interestingly, the distribution of symptoms may help distinguish alternative diagnoses. In our study, weakness and numbness or limb pain were common in patients with alternative diagnoses, whereas fatigue and memory disturbance were more frequently reported in patients with long COVID (Table 3). The heterogeneity of alternative diseases makes it difficult to state definitively that weakness is the most common symptom in patients without long COVID. However, a prior systematic review and meta‐analysis showed that nearly 30% of patients experienced fatigue 12 or more weeks after a COVID‐19 diagnosis [20]. Fatigue is widely recognized as one of the most common symptoms of long COVID, and our clinical experience in Japan supports this observation [10, 21]. Therefore, the absence of fatigue in patients suspected of having long COVID may serve as a key indicator for primary care physicians to consider alternative diagnoses, warranting careful examination and complementary testing.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This study had several limitations that should be noted. It was a single‐center, retrospective study with a relatively small sample size and no control group. Because of the small sample size, statistical analyses such as the Mann–Whitney U‐test and Fisher's exact test were not conducted. The limited sample size may be attributed to the fact that most patients at our clinic were referred by other medical institutions or home doctors, who were expected to have already screened for long COVID. Additionally, patients with respiratory or cardiac symptoms were rare at our clinic because the NCNP specializes in research and medical care for patients with brain or muscular disorders. As a result, patients without neurological symptoms may have been less likely to visit our clinic, introducing a degree of selection bias.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In this study, we reported the basic characteristics of patients who were diagnosed with conditions other than long COVID. Although the proportion of such patients was relatively low, accurate and timely diagnosis remains essential. Our findings suggest plausible candidates for the differential diagnosis of long COVID and highlight that the absence of fatigue may be an important clue for primary care physicians to consider alternative conditions rather than long COVID.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: M.O. and M.T. conceived and designed the study. M.O., T.O., H.K., T.S., and M.T. performed data collection; interpreted the results; reviewed and approved the manuscript. M.O. and T.O. performed the measurements and analyzed the results. M.T. provided technical advice for the analysis. M.O. drafted the manuscript.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: This study was approved by the NCNP Clinical Research Review Committee (No. A2021‐101). In accordance with Japanese laws and regulations, written informed consent was not required because of the retrospective nature of the study.

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: We thank Angela Morben, DVM, ELS, from Edanz (https://jp.edanz.com/ac) for editing a draft of this manuscript.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available on request from the corresponding author. The data are not publicly available due to privacy or ethical restrictions.
